context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 9


Problem 9.1 (20 pts)

The ionosphere can be viewed as a dielectric medium of refractive index
    n = √( 1 − ωp^2/ω^2 )
where ω is the frequency and ωp is the plasma frequency, assumed to be a constant.

(a) Make a plot of the magnitude of the propagation vector |k| as a function of frequency ω for an electromagnetic wave propagating through the ionosphere.
(b) Calculate the phase velocity and group velocity of a radio wave propagating at frequency ω = √2·ωp.
(c) What happens to an electromagnetic disturbance within the ionosphere when ω < ωp?


Problem 9.2 (20 pts)

Consider two polarizers and a quarter wave plate as shown in Figure 1.

[Figure 1: Two polarizers (A and B) each with an "easy" axis at angle θA, θB, and a quarter wave plate with "fast" and "slow" axes at angle θC.]

The two polarizers A and B are first inserted in a line of a light beam as shown in Figure 2. The incoming light beam is moving along the z axis and is linearly polarized along the y direction. The initial light intensity is I0. In this problem you are asked to determine light intensity relative to I0 along the line of the beam. The polarizers are perfect in that they transmit 100% of the light polarized along their "easy" axis and stop all light perpendicular to that axis. The angles of the easy axes of the two polarizers (θA, θB) are measured with respect to the y direction. Note: light intensity is proportional to the time average of E^2: I ∝ <E^2>_t.

[Figure 2: Two polarizers A and B placed in series along the beam (positions a and b), the beam I0 entering along z, polarized along y.]

(a) Find the light intensity Ia of the beam after passing through polarizer A as a function of I0 and θA.
(b) Find the light intensity Ib of the beam after passing through both polarizers as a function of I0, θA and θB.

Polarizer A is now replaced by a quarter wave plate C (see Figure 3). The quarter wave plate affects only the phase of the transmitted light. It has two perpendicular axes, "slow" and "fast". The phase of the light polarized along the "slow" axis is delayed by π/2 with respect to the phase of the light polarized along the "fast" axis. The quarter wave plate is positioned such that the angle of the "fast" axis to the y axis is θC.

[Figure 3: Quarter wave plate C (position c) followed by polarizer B (position b), the beam I0 entering along z, polarized along y.]

(d) Find the light intensity Ic of the beam after passing through quarter plate C as a function of the given parameters.
(e) The intensity of light passing through polarizer B will be affected in general by the presence of the quarter plate. Assume that the easy axis of polarizer B is oriented along the y axis (θB = 0). Find the light intensity Ib of the beam after passing through polarizer B as a function of I0 and θC.


Problem 9.3 (20 pts)

A plane, monochromatic electromagnetic wave travels in the +z direction within a dielectric medium. The wave is linearly polarized with the E field at 30° to the x-axis. The angular frequency of the wave is ω and the dielectric constant of the medium is κe.

(a) Write expressions for Ex(z,t), Ey(z,t), Bx(z,t), By(z,t), in terms of ω, c, κe, E0 where E0 is the amplitude of the electric field.
(b) What is the rate of energy flow per unit area, per unit time which is transported across a surface perpendicular to the direction of propagation?
(c) What is the wave equation for electromagnetic waves traveling within the medium?


Problem 9.4 (20 pts)

An electron executes spiral motion in a magnetic field as shown in Figure 4. Its speed is non-relativistic. The position of the electron is given by:
    r(t) = x̂·α·cos(βt) + ŷ·α·sin(βt) + ẑ·γt

[Figure 4: Spiraling electron — a helical trajectory winding around the z-axis.]

(a) Give the frequency ν, the wavevector k and the polarization state of the radiation observed by a radio astronomer located far away in the following directions (x, y, z) (R ≫ α):
    (i)   (0, 0, R)
    (ii)  (R/√2, 0, R/√2)
    (iii) (R, 0, 0)
(b) Give the complete expression for the electric and magnetic field vectors of the waves observed at point B on the z axis with coordinates (x, y, z) = (0, 0, R) where R ≫ α.
(c) Give an expression for the total energy radiated per second by the electron in terms of the given constants. (Hint: you can use the Larmor formula: Power P = q^2·|a|^2 / (6π·ε0·c^3).)
(d) On a sphere of radius R about the source, what is the ratio between the largest and smallest value of the time-averaged Poynting vector?


Problem 9.5 (20 pts)

A light ray containing electric field vector components parallel (E∥) and perpendicular (E⊥) to the plane of incidence of a glass-air interface is shown in Figure 5.

[Figure 5: Glass-air interface — an incident ray strikes the boundary at Brewster's angle θB, with the reflected and refracted rays making a 90° angle; n_air = 1 above, n_glass below.]

(a) If the ray is incident on the glass at Brewster's angle, show by means of sketches (like what is done for the incident ray in Figure 5) the polarization components of both the reflected and refracted rays.
(b) Give a defining equation for the Brewster angle θB. (Hint: use Snell's law.)
(c) Explain on the basis of the acceleration of charges, in the glass, the direction of polarization of the reflected light. Why is the reflected light polarized parallel to the interface?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem Set 9 overall, especially the ionosphere one and the quarter-wave plate/polarizer stuff. Could we start with 9.1, but maybe in a really broad intuitive way first because I’m not even sure what the formulas are telling me?
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start. Before we dive into the math, tell me this: the refractive index of the ionosphere is given as n = √(1 − ωp²/ω²). What do you think happens to n as ω gets very large (much bigger than ωp)? And what about when ω gets close to ωp? What does that suggest to you physically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad intuitive entry point into Problem 9.1, which is a reasonable starting place. I should use Socratic method to probe what they already know rather than explaining things to them. A single targeted question about what the refractive index formula is telling them physically will activate prior knowledge and guide discovery without giving anything away. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think as \( \omega \gg \omega_p \), \(n\) goes to 1, so it kind of acts like empty space? And when \( \omega \to \omega_p \), \(n \to 0\), but I’m not sure what “index going to zero” is supposed to mean physically. That didn’t fully click for me, and I’m still tempted to want the whole big-picture explanation first.
exchanges.tutor: You've got both limits exactly right - good. Now let's make n→0 click. You know from your course that the propagation vector k is related to n and ω. What is that relationship? Once you write it down, what does k equal when n→0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified both limits, which is good progress. I should affirm this and then push them to connect n→0 to something they already know - specifically the relationship between n and k (the propagation vector). This connects directly to part (a) of the problem. I should resist giving the big-picture explanation they're asking for, and instead keep them moving toward the solution through targeted questions. One question at a time, bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think it’s \(k = n\omega/c\), so if \(n \to 0\) then \(k \to 0\). I’m not really seeing why that matters though, and I feel like I’m missing the overall story of what the medium is doing to the wave.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now think about what k actually represents in a wave. If you write the wave as E ~ e^(ikz), what does a very small k (or even imaginary k) tell you about how the wave behaves as it travels through the medium?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified k = nω/c and the implication k→0. They're asking for the 'overall story' which is a reasonable desire but I shouldn't just explain it - I should push them to reason it out themselves. The key next step is connecting k→0 to what happens to the wave (evanescent/no propagation when ω<ωp, which is part c). I'll ask them to think about what k represents physically to bridge their correct math to physical intuition, staying Socratic and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: A very small \(k\) would mean the phase changes really slowly with \(z\), I think. If \(k\) is imaginary then it stops looking oscillatory and becomes exponential, but I’m shaky on whether that means it just dies out, reflects, or something else.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track - exponential behavior is the key insight. Now look back at k = nω/c and the expression for n. For what values of ω does k become imaginary? That's exactly what part (c) is asking you to address.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they correctly identified that imaginary k means exponential behavior rather than oscillatory. Their uncertainty about whether it dies out or reflects is actually the heart of part (c) of the problem. I should affirm what's right and ask them to think about when k becomes imaginary based on the formula, which will naturally lead them to answer part (c). I don't need to resolve the 'dies out vs reflects' question fully right now - just point them toward using the formula.

exchanges.student: It looks like for \( \omega < \omega_p \), the thing inside the square root is negative, so \(n\) is imaginary and then \(k\) is imaginary too. I guess that means the disturbance becomes evanescent instead of propagating, but I’m still not confident how to say that cleanly, and I haven’t really started part (a) or (b).
exchanges.tutor: That's the right physical picture for part (c) - hold onto that. Now for part (a): you have k = nω/c and you know n as a function of ω. Can you write out |k| explicitly as a function of ω? What does the expression look like, and what are the important 'landmark' points you'd want to mark on the plot?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the condition for evanescent behavior and has essentially answered part (c) conceptually. I should affirm this and redirect them to parts (a) and (b) since they acknowledge they haven't started those. Part (a) asks for a plot of |k| vs ω, which they can now construct from k = nω/c. I'll push them toward that without giving the shape of the plot away.

exchanges.student: I think \( |k|=\frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1-\omega_p^2/\omega^2} \), which simplifies to \( |k|=\frac{1}{c}\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2} \) for \( \omega \ge \omega_p \). For \( \omega<\omega_p \) I’m not sure if I’m supposed to plot nothing, or plot the magnitude anyway even though it’s not propagating. That didn’t work; maybe I’m getting stuck on the plotting convention more than the physics.
exchanges.tutor: Your simplification is correct. For the plot, since the wave doesn't propagate when ω < ωp, |k| = 0 for that region is the standard convention. Mark ωp as your key landmark. Now you have everything for part (a) - move on to part (b). What are the definitions of phase velocity and group velocity in terms of ω and k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived |k| and is now stuck on a plotting detail rather than the physics. This is a minor point that shouldn't derail them. I should briefly clarify this convention point and move them forward to part (b), since dwelling on this plotting detail isn't productive.

exchanges.student: I remember \(v_p=\omega/k\) and \(v_g=d\omega/dk\), but from here I’m worried I’ll just do algebra blindly. Could you just show the full derivation for part (b), including plugging in \( \omega=\sqrt{2}\,\omega_p \)?
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that - working it out yourself is the point. You already have the right formulas and the expression for k(ω). Start with vp = ω/k: just substitute your expression for k and simplify. What do you get? We can check your reasoning as you go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give the full derivation, which I must refuse. However, I should acknowledge their correct formulas and push them to start the process themselves rather than just refusing flatly. I'll remind them of my role and redirect them to take the first step.

exchanges.student: If I substitute \(k=\frac{1}{c}\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2}\), then \(v_p=\frac{\omega}{k}= \frac{c\omega}{\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2}}\). I’m guessing that at \( \omega=\sqrt{2}\,\omega_p \) this becomes \( \sqrt{2}\,c \), but I’m less sure about \(v_g\), and I kind of want the general result too so I can see the pattern.
exchanges.tutor: Check your arithmetic on vp at ω=√2·ωp - plug in carefully and you'll find a different answer. Also, getting a phase velocity greater than c should make you pause and think about whether that's physically reasonable. For vg, try computing dω/dk from your k(ω) expression - or equivalently, find dk/dω first and take the reciprocal. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived the general vp expression but made an arithmetic error when substituting ω=√2·ωp - they got √2·c which is greater than c, which should raise a flag. I should point this out without giving the answer. For vg, I should prompt them to take the derivative themselves rather than providing the general result. Being critical of the arithmetic error is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I think I did plug it in right: \(v_p = c\omega/\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2}\), so at \( \omega=\sqrt{2}\omega_p \) the denominator is just \( \omega_p \), giving \(v_p=\sqrt{2}c\). I’m getting tangled because that seems “too fast,” and for \(v_g\) I’m afraid I’ll mess up the derivative unless I see the whole expression once.
exchanges.tutor: You're right - I was wrong to flag that. Your vp = √2·c is correct, and phase velocity exceeding c is actually physically allowed in a dispersive medium (it doesn't carry information). For vg, just try differentiating k = (1/c)√(ω²−ωp²) with respect to ω. What's dk/dω? Start there and we'll check it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's arithmetic is actually correct - I made an error in my previous response. I need to acknowledge this and not mislead the student. The phase velocity exceeding c is physically valid for a dispersive medium - it's the group velocity that carries information and must be ≤ c. This is an important physical point worth making briefly. Then I should push them to attempt the vg derivative themselves without showing it to them.

exchanges.student: I think \( \dfrac{dk}{d\omega}=\dfrac{1}{c}\cdot \dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2}} \), so \( v_g=\dfrac{d\omega}{dk}=c\,\dfrac{\sqrt{\omega^2-\omega_p^2}}{\omega} \). Then at \( \omega=\sqrt{2}\omega_p \), I get \(v_g=c/\sqrt{2}\). I’m still feeling like I only half understand this, though, and I might need to jump to one of the optics problems because those feel even fuzzier.
exchanges.tutor: Both results are correct - well done. Notice anything interesting about vp · vg? That's worth a moment's thought. Before jumping to the optics problems, you've worked out parts (a), (b), and (c) conceptually - have you written up part (c) as a proper answer yet? If so, we can move to 9.2.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived both vg and vp for part (b) and also correctly evaluated them at ω=√2·ωp. This is good progress. I should affirm this and point out the interesting relationship between vp and vg (their product equals c²) as a prompt for reflection, without lecturing. Then I should acknowledge their desire to move on but first check if they've addressed part (c) in writing, since we discussed it conceptually but they haven't formally written it up. I'll also note the pattern vp·vg = c² as a Socratic nudge rather than an explanation.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student step-by-step without giving away answers. When the student explicitly asked for a full derivation in Turn 7, the tutor firmly declined and redirected. The tutor built on each correct student response, acknowledged errors (including its own in Turn 9), and kept responses concise and focused. No significant rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Each turn posed a targeted question building on the student's prior response. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps (e.g., 'Your simplification is correct,' 'Both results are correct') and built directly on them rather than restarting. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, e.g., advising the student to 'plug in carefully' and noting that vp > c 'should make you pause,' which is method-level guidance. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily through parts (a), (b), and (c) without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student stayed on task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to Problem 9.1. 3.1: Tutor responses were concise and interactive, rarely exceeding a short paragraph. 3.2: Tone was supportive and non-evaluative throughout, with no grading language used.
